subject: What if you avoid a visit from a noble if no one notices?

posts.body:
IF NO ONE NOTICE, and you refuse the visit from a noble, is considered a cheat?
How do you prevent that from happening?
Do you make it a MANDATORY ACTION before the end of the turn to check if a noble visits you?

I ask this because it could be that with the visit of a noble YOU TRIGGER THE LAST ROUND WITH EXACTLY 15 POINTS, and you will no longer
have the opportunity to buy a development card with a higher score than the noble's, and your opponent can win.

An apology for so many edits to the original post, I didn't know how to explain myself very well from the beginning.

Thanks!

I'm not familiar with the term "visit a nobel"  ... I always learned it's "claiming a nobel"

but it's optional to not claim one... and it is required to not claim one if you are claiming another one the same term... ie. you can claim at most 1 nobel per turn

edit: why wouldn't you want to claim a noble if you can?  you do run risk if you aren't paying attention for another player to claim it out from under you....

Rules explicitly say you can't refuse a noble you've earned.

A player cannot refuse a visit from a noble.

Pete (thinks that's pretty clear-cut)

plezercruz wrote:

Rules explicitly say you can't refuse a noble you've earned.

interesting... except of course in the case where you've "earned" two or possibly more on same turn I thought for sure you can't claim more than one per turn?

JeffyJeff wrote:

plezercruz wrote:

Rules explicitly say you can't refuse a noble you've earned.

interesting... except of course in the case where you've "earned" two or possibly more on same turn I thought for sure you can't claim more than one per turn?

Yes, that's right.  You can only take one per turn.

Pete (nods)

Rules explicitly say
[q=""]A player cannot refuse a visit from a noble.

Receiving a noble isn't considered to be an action. Each noble tile is worth 3 prestige points, but players can only get a single one per turn.

JeffyJeff wrote:

plezercruz wrote:

Rules explicitly say you can't refuse a noble you've earned.

interesting... except of course in the case where you've "earned" two or possibly more on same turn I thought for sure you can't claim more than one per turn?

Correct. If you fulfill two nobles at the same time, then you take just one and you must wait till your next turn to take the other, always than another player doesn't earn it in that window.

🥷

plezercruz wrote:

What a weird edit...

Pete (shrugs)

hahaha sorry! I have ADHD and many times I have a hard time explaining myself well on the first try.

plezercruz wrote:

JeffyJeff wrote:

plezercruz wrote:

Rules explicitly say you can't refuse a noble you've earned.

interesting... except of course in the case where you've "earned" two or possibly more on same turn I thought for sure you can't claim more than one per turn?

Yes, that's right.  You can only take one per turn.

fwiw it's still news to me as I didn't read that rule apparently that it's required to table a noble if you can... especially since some players may not even notice they've earned one (or more).

I also see only one case why you wouldn't want to claim one if you knew you've earned one on your turn except in the boundary case claiming that noble triggers end game and you believe you won't win by triggering game end then as you need possibly just one more turn to get more points to beat someone who plays after you in turn order.

JeffyJeff wrote:

plezercruz wrote:

JeffyJeff wrote:

plezercruz wrote:

Rules explicitly say you can't refuse a noble you've earned.

interesting... except of course in the case where you've "earned" two or possibly more on same turn I thought for sure you can't claim more than one per turn?

Yes, that's right.  You can only take one per turn.

fwiw it's still news to me as I didn't read that rule apparently that it's required to table a noble if you can... especially since some players may not even notice they've earned one (or more).

I also see only one case why you wouldn't want to claim one if you knew you've earned one on your turn except in the boundary case claiming that noble triggers end game and you believe you won't win by triggering game end then as you need possibly just one more turn to get more points to beat someone who plays after you in turn order.

I've never been in a situation where I'd pass up the points.  If I have an opportunity to claim a noble that would put me over 15 or not, that usually has no bearing on whether another player will score later on that turn and trigger endgame himself.   And if I'm the last player in a round and I can trigger an endgame, that means I win.  

I can conceive of situations where I would not want to buy the card that gets me the noble (on the off chance that the newly flipped card gives an opponent more points) but having bought a card and flipped a new one, I can't imagine passing on a noble.

Pete (suspects the rule is there for a reason though, and that the original poster found it)

Looking at some other threads on the topic, notably this one: https://boardgamegeek.com/thread/1572740/splendor-nobles-wha...

Gabor B. @uborka_original suggests the rule is there so that if you forget to take a noble you are entitled to, other players can't claim you chose not to take it.

Pete (thinks that makes sense)

OrcGolem wrote:

I ask this because it could be that with the visit of a noble YOU TRIGGER THE LAST ROUND WITH EXACTLY 15 POINTS, and you will no longer
have the opportunity to buy a development card with a higher score than the noble's, and your opponent can win.

I do not think that's a valid reason to pass on a noble even if you could per the rules. There are only two options how this round can play out:

a) Someone is in turn order before you. They either will already have 15+ points which means it is the last round. Or they have fewer than 15 which means you will win with the noble. 

b) Someone is in turn order after you which means they can either not overtake you in VP or gain 16 or more points which ends the game anyway without giving you another turn.  

Maybe you are forgetting that all players get an even number of turns, meaning the last player will be next to the starting player, not the one next to the player ending the game?

plezercruz wrote:

suggests the rule is there so that if you forget to take a noble you are entitled to, other players can't claim you chose not to take it.

so other players when it gets to their turn and could claim the nobel has to point out and give it to the player who should have claimed it?

not sure how that would work in tournament play 🙂 playing "friendly" sure.

then you get into the trickier situation where they have 2 nobles they could claim on a turn one of which  another player is one tableau card away from claiming and you can see they have the gems and card is available for purchase that would give it to them.... and the other noble no one is close to claiming... and the player claims the later.  Are the other players here also required to point that out to the player?  

Seems like such a rule should only be in the "family friendly variant" section

Maybe you are forgetting that all players get an even number of turns, meaning the last player will be next to the starting player, not the one next to the player ending the game?

Hi!,

The rules in spanish say:
(and my confusion starts with the Game Start part, cuz I don't understant that like a "THE ROUND starts with..." 🥲)

Game Start part: "THE GAME starts with the youngest player and proceeds clockwise".
Game Over part: "When a player has 15 points (don't forget to count the nobles), THE ROUND in play continues, so that all players play the same number of turns."

Maybe I take (for my very notorious lack of reading comprehension) "THE ROUND in play" as from when a player gets the 15 points (or more) that trigger the end of the game and for me all the players take one last turn even if I'm the player at the right from the player who starts "the game" or... "the round..." x_x.

OrcGolem wrote:

Hi!,

The rules in spanish say:
(and my confusion starts with the Game Start part, cuz I don't understant that like a "THE ROUND starts with..." 🥲)

Game Start part: "THE GAME starts with the youngest player and proceeds clockwise".
Game Over part: "When a player has 15 points (don't forget to count the nobles), THE ROUND in play continues, so that all players play the same number of turns."

Maybe I take (for my very notorious lack of reading comprehension) "THE ROUND in play" as from when a player gets the 15 points (or more) that trigger the end of the game and for me all the players take one last turn even if I'm the player at the right from the player who starts "the game" or... "the round..." x_x.

A note on terminology: In Splendor, a *Round* consists of all players taking a single *Turn*.  This is mostly consistent through most board games, but is not always the case.

Say you are playing a 3-player game, with players A, B, and C.  Player A goes first, followed by Player B, and Player C goes last.

Say, Player B triggers the end game by scoring 15 points on their 12th turn.  That means you will finish out the round and the game is over.  Player C will get one more turn (as it will be their 12th turn), and scores 16 points.

Player A doesn't get another turn, since they already took their 12th turn during the 12th and final round before Player B triggered end-game.

End result:  Player A finishes with fewer than 15 points on 12 turns.  Player B finishes with exactly 15 points on their 12th turn.  Player C wins the game by passing Player B in scoring on their final (12th) turn.

Games don't always last 12 rounds, but that seems to be somewhere around the average when I play the app.  Some games end in 10 rounds (or less).  Some end in 14 rounds (or more).  But the main takeaway is that you compare scores after all players have played the same number of rounds.

--As an aside, when playing the app and you reach the prerequisites to earn two Nobles on the same turn, it shows you which two Nobles you can claim, and you must choose one of them.  You will earn the other one automatically at the end of your next turn (provided someone else doesn't claim it before you).  So it is always good to look at other players' tableaus and first choose the Noble that other players are closer earning, so that the second Noble will most likely still be there next round for you to claim.  It does not let you *not* take a Noble that you have earned.

In brief: The player sitting to the right of the youngest player will always take the last turn of the game. (Assuming your started the game according to the rules.) 

For scoring, it does not matter where in turn order the player ending the game is as everyone gets the same number of turns (which will equal the number of rounds played).

It does not let you *not* take a Noble that you have earned.

Hi! Thanks! and yeah I understand that about the rounds, I have seen it in other games as you mention, but the wording of the rules in Spanish for Splendor confused me “a bit”haha.
So from now on I'll interpret it that way 😀

odie73 wrote:

(Assuming your started the game according to the rules.) 

almost never... for gaves with a start player advantage or disadvantage it will always be the same start player each time for a given game within a small play group.
